name: Akira Ransomware — Active Intrusion
description: Akira ransomware via Cisco VPN CVE-2020-3259. Mimikatz credential dump, RDP lateral movement, AnyDesk C2, WinSCP exfiltration, shadow copy deletion, .akira encryption. MITRE: T1133, T1003.001, T1021.001, T1219, T1048, T1490, T1486.

prelude.title: {{customerName}} — Akira Ransomware Intrusion
prelude.subtitle: A double-extortion attack against {{customerName}} using Akira ransomware. The attackers spent 9 days inside the network collecting data before deploying encryption — exactly the kind of high-consequence event Sophos is built to surface and stop.
prelude.transitionLine: Now let's pivot into Sophos Central and show exactly what your team at {{customerName}} would see — the alerts, case context, detections, and the response path that turns this story into action before business impact.
expectedDetections: Cisco VPN authentication anomaly flagged on an account not seen in 47 days
LSASS memory access by a non-system process — Mimikatz credential dump detected
AnyDesk installation by a non-admin user outside business hours
WinSCP SFTP session to an external IP transferring 47GB over 6 hours
Shadow copy deletion via vssadmin — classic Akira ransomware precursor
CryptoGuard blocks .akira encryption attempt — 2,847 files protected

mitreTechniques.name: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools

slides.title: Day 1: Akira gains access through {{customerName}}'s VPN
slides.body: Attackers exploit CVE-2020-3259 in {{customerName}}'s Cisco ASA firewall — extracting plaintext VPN credentials directly from device memory. No phishing. No malware. Eight employee accounts compromised silently. The attackers authenticate as legitimate users and the VPN logs show nothing unusual.

slides.title: Days 2–8: Silent collection inside {{customerName}}'s network
slides.body: The attackers dump credentials from LSASS using Mimikatz, move laterally via RDP to file servers and backup systems, install AnyDesk for persistent C2, and exfiltrate 47GB of patient records, financial data, and HR files via WinSCP before anyone notices.

slides.title: Day 9: Encryption — Sophos changes the outcome
slides.body: Akira deletes shadow copies, stops backup services, and deploys the ransomware binary. Sophos detects the shadow copy deletion, CryptoGuard blocks the encryption attempt, and gives the team at {{customerName}} an active investigation with full attack context — before the business crisis begins.

talkingPoints: Akira is a double-extortion group — they steal data first, then encrypt. Even if you restore from backup, they still publish your data.
This attack started with a VPN vulnerability, not a phishing email. Perimeter tools alone don't catch this.
The 9-day dwell time is typical for Akira. The question is whether your team sees it on day 1 or day 9.
What we're about to show in Central is the difference between an alert and an actionable investigation that tells your team exactly what happened and what to do.

healthcare: For {{customerName}}, the 47GB exfiltrated by Akira likely includes patient records — that's a HIPAA breach event before the encryption even starts.
When clinical systems encrypt, the cost isn't just downtime — it's delayed care, diverted patients, and regulatory exposure.
financial_services: Akira's data exfiltration before encryption means {{customerName}} faces both ransomware recovery costs and regulatory breach notification requirements.
The dwell time alone — 9 days undetected — is a material compliance failure under most financial services frameworks.
manufacturing: For {{customerName}}, a 9-day undetected intrusion means the attackers likely have full visibility into production systems, OT network topology, and supplier relationships.
Encryption of operational systems doesn't just stop IT — it stops the production floor.

runbookSteps: Open with the business stakes, not the technical details — for healthcare, lead with patient data and HIPAA.
Tell the 3-beat story: VPN access → silent collection → encryption attempt stopped.
Use the expected detections as the promise of what the stakeholder is about to see in Central.
The Win11 simulation shows what an infected machine looks like — then pivot to Central to show Sophos had the answer before it got there.
Close on the response outcome: Sophos stopped the encryption, preserved the evidence trail, and gave the team a path to act immediately.
clickPath: Open the high-severity alert — show the shadow copy deletion that fired as Akira prepared to encrypt. This is the moment that matters.
Move into the investigation/case view — show how Sophos correlates 9 days of attacker activity into a single coherent incident, not 40 disconnected alerts.
Show the detection chain: VPN anomaly → LSASS dump → AnyDesk → WinSCP exfil → shadow copy deletion → CryptoGuard block.
Close on the response actions: isolation, forensic preservation, and the clear path your team at {{customerName}} would take to contain and recover.

items.description: CryptoGuard blocked Akira ransomware encryption on SRV-FILESTORE01. 2,847 files protected. Ransom note 'akira_readme.txt' drop attempted.
items.info: Akira ransomware behavior detected and blocked by CryptoGuard

items.description: Shadow copy deletion detected on SRV-FILESTORE01. Command: vssadmin delete shadows /all /quiet. Executed by: svchost_upd.exe (PID 7742). Ransomware precursor behavior.
LSASS memory access by non-system process on DESKTOP-FIN042. Process: svchost_upd.exe accessing lsass.exe (PID 872). Mimikatz credential dumping pattern detected.
items.info: OS Credential Dumping: LSASS Memory — T1003.001

items.description: AnyDesk remote access software installed outside business hours on DESKTOP-FIN042. User: sarah.chen. Installation source: C:\Users\sarah.chen\AppData\Local\Temp\AnyDesk.exe. Not authorized by IT policy.
Large outbound data transfer detected from SRV-BACKUP02. WinSCP process transferred 47.3GB via SFTP to 185.220.101.47 (Tor exit node) over 6.2 hours. Possible exfiltration — T1048.002.
Attempt to disable Windows Defender real-time protection on SRV-FILESTORE01 blocked by Tamper Protection. Process: svchost_upd.exe (PID 7742).
items.info: Tamper Protection blocked defense evasion attempt

items.name: (SRV-FILESTORE01) | Akira Ransomware — {{customerName}} — Active Intrusion
items.overview: Akira ransomware group active inside {{customerName}} network for an estimated 9 days. Initial access via Cisco ASA/FTD CVE-2020-3259. Credential dump from LSASS on DESKTOP-FIN042. Lateral movement via RDP to 6 additional hosts including SRV-FILESTORE01 and SRV-BACKUP02. AnyDesk C2 channel maintained for 6 days. 47.3GB exfiltrated via WinSCP to Tor exit node 185.220.101.47. Shadow copies deleted across 4 servers. CryptoGuard blocked encryption on SRV-FILESTORE01 — 2,847 files protected. Ransomware binary: svchost_upd.exe (masquerading as Windows service). akira_readme.txt drop blocked.

extraActivities.action: CryptoGuard blocked Akira encryption on SRV-FILESTORE01. 2,847 files protected. Ransom note drop (akira_readme.txt) blocked.
Tamper Protection blocked attempt to disable Windows Defender on SRV-FILESTORE01. Process: svchost_upd.exe (PID 7742).
SRV-FILESTORE01 isolated from network. Threat confirmed: Akira ransomware. Incident escalated to IR team.
Ransomware binary quarantined: C:\ProgramData\upd\svchost_upd.exe (SHA256: 3b4c5d6e...a3b4c). AnyDesk C2 process terminated on DESKTOP-FIN042.
Large data transfer alert correlated to case. WinSCP transferred 47.3GB from SRV-BACKUP02 to 185.220.101.47 (Tor exit node) over 6.2 hours — 2 days prior to encryption attempt.

items.description: SRV-FILESTORE01 isolated from network following Akira ransomware detection. Threat actor: CONTOSO\svc_backup (compromised account).
CryptoGuard blocked Akira ransomware file encryption. 2,847 files protected. Ransom note akira_readme.txt drop blocked. Ransomware binary svchost_upd.exe (SHA256: 3b4c5d6e…) quarantined.
Akira ransomware attempted to delete all VSS shadow copies via encoded PowerShell. Sophos detected and flagged the activity. vssadmin delete shadows /all /quiet — T1490.
svchost_upd.exe accessed lsass.exe process memory on DESKTOP-FIN042. Mimikatz credential dump pattern. Domain admin credentials at risk. User: CONTOSO\sarah.chen.

VPN authentication using account sarah.chen from 185.220.101.47. Account not seen in 47 days. Credentials likely extracted from Cisco ASA device memory via CVE-2020-3259. No brute force or spray pattern detected — consistent with Akira credential harvesting. Initial intrusion point.

queryResults.query: SELECT pid, name, path, cmdline, parent FROM processes WHERE name LIKE '%svchost_upd%' OR name LIKE '%AnyDesk%' OR name LIKE '%WinSCP%' OR cmdline LIKE '%vssadmin%' OR cmdline LIKE '%lsass%' ORDER BY start_time DESC
queryResults.queryName: Akira Ransomware IOC Hunt — Active Processes
rows: net stop vss & net stop wbengine & net stop swprv

alert.description: 🔒 SRV-FILESTORE01 automatically isolated by Sophos. Akira ransomware contained. All network connections terminated. 2,847 files protected from encryption.
